messages.content:
I tried to run a python file through excel vba. I used 2 methods. 

Sub RunPythonCode()
    Shell "C:\path\to\python.exe C:\path\to\pythonfile\test.py", vbNormalFocus    
End Sub

It works well.But once I changed the code to  

Sub RunPythonCode()
    RunPython ("import test; test.my_function()")
End Sub

it tells me "еӯҗиҝҮзЁӢжҲ–еҮҪж•°жңӘе®ҡд№ү"пјҢ could you help me troubleshooting? thanks.

The error message "еӯҗиҝҮзЁӢжҲ–еҮҪж•°жңӘе®ҡд№ү" (which translates to "subroutine or function is not defined") suggests that the VBA code is unable to find the "RunPython" subroutine.

The "RunPython" subroutine is not a built-in function in Excel VBA, so you need to define it yourself or use an external library that provides this functionality.

One popular library for running Python code from VBA is called pyxll. You can download and install it, and then add a reference to the pyxll library in your VBA code.

Here is an example of how you could use pyxll to run Python code from VBA:

Sub RunPythonCode()
    ' Create Python object
    Dim python As Object
    Set python = CreateObject("Python.Runtime.Python")

    ' Call Python function
    python.Run "import test; test.my_function()"

    ' Clean up Python object
    Set python = Nothing
End Sub

Note that you will need to adjust the import statement and the function call to match your specific Python code.

Alternatively, you can continue using the Shell method to directly call the Python interpreter, as you mentioned it was working well for you.
I have xlwings installed which also provides this functionality right?

Yes, that's correct. If you already have xlwings installed, you can use it to run Python code from VBA. Here's an example:

Sub RunPythonCode()
    ' Import xlwings module
    Import xlwings as xw

    ' Create a reference to the currently active Excel application
    Dim app As Object
    Set app = xw.Application

    ' Run Python code
    app.RunPython "import test; test.my_function()"

    ' Clean up
    Set app = Nothing
End Sub

Make sure that you have the xlwings library properly installed and added as a reference in your VBA project.

Once you've set up xlwings, you can use the "app.RunPython" method to execute Python code. Adjust the import statement and function call to match your specific Python code.

Note that xlwings also has other features that allow bidirectional communication between Excel and Python, such as passing data between Excel and Python, and controlling Excel from Python. So if you need more advanced integration between Excel and Python, xlwings can be a good option.

вҖҷ Import xlwings module
Import xlwings as xw   зј–иҜ‘й”ҷиҜҜ иҜӯжі•й”ҷиҜҜ

Apologies for the confusion. In VBA, the correct syntax for importing a module is using the "Declare" statement. Here's the adjusted code for importing the xlwings module:

Sub RunPythonCode()
    ' Declare xlwings module
    Declare Sub RunPython Lib "xlwings.dll" (ByVal code As String)

    ' Run Python code
    RunPython "import test; test.my_function()"
End Sub

By using the "Declare" statement, you can directly call the "RunPython" subroutine from the xlwings module. Ensure that you have the correct path to the xlwings.dll file specified in the "Lib" argument.

Please note that when using xlwings, you need to have the xlwings Python package installed and configured properly. Additionally, make sure that you have the xlwings.dll file in a location where VBA can access it.
иӢҘиҰҒеңЁ64дҪҚзі»з»ҹдёҠдҪҝз”ЁпјҢеҲҷеҝ…йЎ»жӣҙж–°жӯӨйЎ№зӣ®дёӯзҡ„д»Јз ҒгҖӮиҜ·жЈҖжҹҘе№¶жӣҙж–°declareиҜӯеҸҘпјҢз„¶еҗҺз”ЁPtrSafeеұһжҖ§ж Үи®°е®ғд»¬

Certainly! If you're working on a 64-bit system, you'll need to update the VBA code with the PtrSafe attribute. Here's the updated code:

#If VBA7 Then
    Private Declare PtrSafe Sub RunPython Lib "xlwings.dll" (ByVal code As String)
#Else
    Private Declare Sub RunPython Lib "xlwings.dll" (ByVal code As String)
#End If

Sub RunPythonCode()
    ' Run Python code
    RunPython "import test; test.my_function()"
End Sub

In the updated code, we use the `#If VBA7 Then` conditional compilation directive to handle the different declare statement for 64-bit systems. When VBA is running on a 64-bit version, it will use the Declare statement with the `PtrSafe` attribute.

Make sure that you have the correct path to the `xlwings.dll` file specified in the `Lib` argument.

Remember to have the xlwings Python package installed and configured properly, and ensure that you have the `xlwings.dll` file in a location where VBA can access it.
my system is 64-bit, so no need to write a if condition, please help me revise.

Apologies for the confusion. If your system is already 64-bit, then you don't need to include the conditional compilation directive. Here's the updated code:

Private Declare PtrSafe Sub RunPython Lib "xlwings.dll" (ByVal code As String)

Sub RunPythonCode()
    ' Run Python code
    RunPython "import test; test.my_function()"
End Sub

In this updated code, we use the `PtrSafe` attribute for the `Declare` statement. This ensures that the VBA code can run correctly on 64-bit systems.

Make sure you have the correct path to the `xlwings.dll` file specified in the `Lib` argument, and ensure that the `xlwings` Python package is installed and configured properly. Also, please ensure that the `xlwings.dll` file is in a location accessible to VBA.
Can we put the declare before sub?

Yes, you can put the `Declare` statement before the `Sub`. Here's the revised code:

Private Declare PtrSafe Sub RunPython Lib "xlwings.dll" (ByVal code As String)

Sub RunPythonCode()
    ' Run Python code
    RunPython "import test; test.my_function()"
End Sub

By placing the `Declare` statement before the `Sub`, you ensure that the `RunPython` subroutine is properly declared and can be used within the `Sub`. This arrangement is common and follows best practices for organizing VBA code.

Remember to adjust the path to the `xlwings.dll` file in the `Lib` argument and ensure that the `xlwings` Python package is installed correctly.
you are right. It shows"иҝҗиЎҢж—¶й”ҷиҜҜ"53" ж–Үд»¶жңӘжүҫеҲ°:xlwings.dll"

The "иҝҗиЎҢж—¶й”ҷиҜҜ 53" (Runtime Error 53) indicates that the `xlwings.dll` file was not found in the specified location. 

Here are a few things you can try to resolve the issue:

1. Check the file path: Double-check the location of the `xlwings.dll` file. Make sure you have the correct file path specified in the `Lib` argument of the `Declare` statement. If necessary, provide the full absolute path to the `xlwings.dll` file.

2. Verify the file name: Ensure that the file name is spelled correctly as "xlwings.dll". The error can occur if the file name is misspelled or if the file extension is incorrect.

3. Reinstall xlwings: Try reinstalling the xlwings Python package and ensure that it is installed correctly. Refer to the xlwings documentation for the proper installation instructions.

4. Check the file location: Make sure the `xlwings.dll` file is located in a directory accessible to VBA. One option is to place the DLL file in the same directory as your Excel file. Alternatively, you can try placing it in a directory already listed in the system's PATH environment variable.

By resolving the file not found error, you should be able to successfully run the VBA code that interacts with xlwings.
where should I find the xlwings.dll? my xlwings libarary is installed in the following path C:\Users\LIQI\anaconda3\Lib\site-packages\xlwings

In your case, the `xlwings.dll` file should be present within the `xlwings` package folder. Assuming that your `xlwings` library is installed in the path `C:\Users\LIQI\anaconda3\Lib\site-packages\xlwings`, you will typically find the `xlwings.dll` file in the `C:\Users\LIQI\anaconda3\Lib\site-packages\xlwings\xlwings` directory.

Ensure that you have the correct path to the `xlwings.dll` file specified in the `Lib` argument of the `Declare` statement. In your VBA code, it should be:

Private Declare PtrSafe Sub RunPython Lib "C:\Users\LIQI\anaconda3\Lib\site-packages\xlwings\xlwings.dll" (ByVal code As String)

Replace `C:\Users\LIQI\anaconda3\Lib\site-packages\xlwings\xlwings.dll` with the actual path to the `xlwings.dll` file on your system.

Remember to include the appropriate backslashes `\` in the file path, and make sure that the DLL file exists in the specified location.
I found the dll file. It's in the same path with python.exe. the file name is xlwings64-0.24.9.dll

If you have found the `xlwings64-0.24.9.dll` file in the same location as your `python.exe` file, you should update the `Lib` argument in the `Declare` statement accordingly.

Try using the following code:

Private Declare PtrSafe Sub RunPython Lib "C:\path\to\python.exe\xlwings64-0.24.9.dll" (ByVal code As String)

Replace "C:\path\to\python.exe" with the actual path to your `python.exe` file, including the file name. Update the file path according to your system setup.

Make sure that the `xlwings64-0.24.9.dll` file exists in the specified location. Also, ensure that the path you provide points to the correct `python.exe` file.

If you encounter any further issues, double-check that `xlwings` is properly installed and confirm the location of the DLL file.
жүҫдёҚеҲ°dllиҫ“е…ҘзӮ№RunPythonпјҢеңЁC:\Users\LIQI\anaconda3\xlwings64-0.24.9.dllдёӯ

If you are encountering an error stating that the entry point for `RunPython` couldn't be found in the `xlwings64-0.24.9.dll` file, it's possible that the `xlwings` library you have installed does not contain the necessary functions for VBA integration.

To resolve this issue, you can try the following steps:

1. Ensure `xlwings` installation: Double-check that you have the correct version of `xlwings` installed. Visit the xlwings documentation and make sure you have followed the installation instructions accurately.

2. Verify DLL name and location: Confirm that the DLL file path you specified in the `Lib` argument is correct and accessible. Ensure that the DLL file is indeed located at `C:\Users\LIQI\anaconda3\xlwings64-0.24.9.dll` and update the path accordingly if needed.

3. Use the correct DLL file: Consider searching for the correct DLL file name to use with `xlwings` and VBA integration. Check the `xlwings` documentation or the installation folder to find the appropriate DLL file for VBA integration.

4. Consult `xlwings` documentation and community: If the issue persists, it may be helpful to refer to the `xlwings` documentation or consult the `xlwings` community for further assistance. They may have specific instructions or troubleshooting steps to address this particular issue.

By following these steps and ensuring proper installation and correct DLL file usage, you should be able to resolve the error related to the `RunPython` entry point not being found.
IвҖҳm comfused. I have 2 excel files and 2 conresponding python file.  One can use the runpython order in vba, and the other cannot not... I'm not convinced that the issue is related to the dll file.

I apologize for any confusion caused. If you have two sets of Excel files and corresponding Python files, and one set can successfully use the `RunPython` order in VBA while the other cannot, it is possible that the issue may not be related to the DLL file itself.

In this case, it's important to investigate other potential factors that could be causing the problem. Here are a few troubleshooting steps for further investigation:

1. Verify Python environment: Confirm that both Excel files are using the same Python environment and version. Make sure that the required Python libraries, including `xlwings`, are installed and configured correctly for both environments.

2. Check VBA project references: In the Excel file where the `RunPython` order is not working, go to the VBA editor and check the references in the project. Make sure that all required references are properly set and not displaying any errors.

3. Check syntax and function names: Review the Python code in the corresponding Python files. Ensure that the syntax is correct, and double-check the names of the functions being called in the `RunPython` order to ensure they match the actual functions in the Python file without any spelling or case sensitivity issues.

4. Debugging Python code: Add some print statements or logging statements in the Python code to check if it is executing properly or encountering any errors. This will help in debugging and identifying any potential issues within the Python code itself.

5. Test with a minimal example: To isolate the issue, try creating a minimal example where you have a simple Python function called by the `RunPython` order in VBA. This will help determine if the problem lies with the specific code or if there are external factors involved.

By following these troubleshooting steps, you can narrow down the potential causes of the issue and hopefully resolve the problem with the `RunPython` order in VBA for the Excel file where it is not currently working.
Yes. I do test with the minimal example and goes wrong... the file that works well is a much more complex one...

If you have tested with a minimal example and it still doesn't work as expected, even though a more complex file is functioning correctly, there may be additional factors that need to be considered. Here are a few suggestions to further troubleshoot the issue:

1. Check dependencies: Verify if there are any external dependencies or libraries that are required for the Python code to work properly. Ensure that these dependencies are properly installed and configured in the environment where the minimal example is being tested.

2. Review Python environment: Compare the Python environment configurations between the two Excel files. Check if they are using different versions of Python or different virtual environments. Make sure that both environments have the necessary packages installed for running xlwings and any other required libraries.

3. Error handling: Add proper error handling in the Python code and VBA code to capture any potential errors or exceptions that may be occurring during the execution. Monitor any error messages or traceback information to identify and address specific issues.

4. Debugging techniques: Utilize debugging techniques such as print statements, logging, or using a Python debugger to step through the code line by line. This can be helpful in identifying any specific point of failure or unexpected behavior.

5. Consider external factors: Assess if there are any external factors that could be affecting the behavior of the minimal example. For example, if the Python code interacts with external files or resources, ensure that the paths or connections are correctly configured and accessible.

6. Seek external help: If the issue persists, consider reaching out to the xlwings support community or forums for assistance. They may have encountered similar issues or have specific insights to help address the problem.

By examining these factors and implementing the suggested troubleshooting steps, you should be able to pinpoint the cause of the issue and resolve the problem with the `RunPython` order in VBA for the minimal example.
Forget it. I will go to sleep. It just take me too long time...

I understand that troubleshooting issues can be frustrating and time-consuming. It's perfectly fine to take a break and get some rest. Sometimes, stepping away from the problem and returning with a fresh perspective can lead to a solution.

If you have further questions or need assistance in the future, feel free to ask. Have a restful sleep, and I hope you're able to resolve the issue when you return to it. Good luck!
thanks for your help.byebye.
You're welcome! I'm glad I could help. If you have any more questions in the future, don't hesitate to ask. Goodbye and take care!
